questions.question:
Which of the following transport layer protocols is used to support electronic mail?

A. SMTP  B. IP  C. TCP  D. UDP
questions.to_find: Which transport layer protocol supports electronic mail (SMTP)?

understand.plain: SMTP is an application-layer protocol for sending email, but it relies on a transport-layer protocol to carry its messages reliably between mail servers. TCP provides the reliable, connection-oriented transport that SMTP requires to guarantee delivery of email data. IP is a network-layer protocol, and UDP is connectionless and unreliable, making it unsuitable for email. SMTP always runs over TCP port 25.

keywords.explain: Transmission Control Protocol - a reliable, connection-oriented transport layer protocol that guarantees delivery and ordering of data segments.
keywords.example: SMTP uses TCP port 25 to reliably transfer email messages between mail servers.

keywords.explain: Simple Mail Transfer Protocol - an application-layer protocol for sending and routing email between mail servers.
keywords.example: When you send an email, your mail client uses SMTP running over TCP to deliver it to the mail server.

keywords.explain: User Datagram Protocol - a connectionless, unreliable transport protocol that provides no delivery guarantee.
keywords.example: DNS queries use UDP because they are small and can tolerate retransmission, whereas email cannot.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' font-family='system-ui,sans-serif' font-size='13'><rect width='320' height='180' fill='var(--color-bg,#f8f8f6)' rx='8'/><text x='160' y='22' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'>Protocol Layers for Email</text><rect x='80' y='35' width='160' height='30' rx='4' fill='var(--color-accent,#4A90D9)' opacity='0.85'/><text x='160' y='55' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff' font-weight='bold'>Application: SMTP</text><rect x='80' y='75' width='160' height='30' rx='4' fill='#E87040' opacity='0.85'/><text x='160' y='95' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff' font-weight='bold'>Transport: TCP (port 25)</text><rect x='80' y='115' width='160' height='30' rx='4' fill='#888' opacity='0.7'/><text x='160' y='135' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff' font-weight='bold'>Network: IP</text><text x='160' y='165' text-anchor='middle' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' font-size='11'>SMTP depends on TCP for reliable delivery</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Protocol stack showing SMTP at application layer sitting on top of TCP at transport layer, which sits on IP at network layer.

given.aim: Identify the transport layer protocol that supports electronic mail (SMTP).

terms.meaning: Layer 4 of the OSI model responsible for end-to-end communication between hosts.
terms.example: TCP and UDP are both transport-layer protocols.
terms.connects: SMTP at the application layer relies on a transport-layer protocol for message delivery.

terms.meaning: SMTP uses TCP port 25 for reliable, ordered delivery of email commands and data.
terms.example: A mail server accepts SMTP connections on TCP port 25 from sending mail servers.
terms.connects: TCP's reliability is what makes SMTP work correctly across the internet.

given.plan: SMTP is an application-layer protocol that needs reliable, ordered delivery of its commands and data. TCP provides this reliability at the transport layer. Therefore TCP is the transport layer protocol supporting electronic mail.

steps.title: Eliminate non-transport protocols
steps.apply: SMTP is application layer (not transport). IP is network layer. Both are eliminated from consideration.
steps.note: The question specifically asks for a transport layer protocol.

steps.title: Choose between TCP and UDP
steps.apply: Email requires reliable, in-order delivery of messages. TCP is connection-oriented and reliable; UDP is connectionless and unreliable. SMTP uses TCP port 25.
steps.note: Any protocol requiring guaranteed delivery uses TCP, not UDP.

questions.formula_note: SMTP runs over TCP at the transport layer because TCP provides the reliable, connection-oriented delivery required for email transmission.

questions.question:
Suppose two hosts use a TCP connection to transfer a large file. Which of the following statements is/are FALSE with respect to the TCP connection?

I. The sequence number of a segment is the sequence number of the first byte of data in the segment; the sequence number of the next segment is always mn + 1, where mn is the sequence number of the current segment.
II. If the estimated round trip time at any given point of time is f sec, the value of the retransmission timeout is always set to greater than or equal to f sec.
III. The number of unacknowledged bytes at the sender is always less than or equal to the receiver's advertised window.
IV. The number of unacknowledged bytes at the sender is always less than or equal to the minimum of the advertised window and the congestion window.

A. III only  B. I and III only  C. I and IV only  D. II and IV only
questions.to_find: Which of the four statements about TCP are FALSE?

understand.plain: TCP sequence numbers represent byte positions, not segment numbers. The next segment's sequence number is the current sequence number plus the number of bytes in the current segment, not always +1. The retransmission timeout (RTO) is set to EstimatedRTT + 4*DevRTT, which is always >= the estimated RTT. The sender's unacknowledged bytes are bounded by BOTH the advertised window (rwnd) and the congestion window (cwnd); Statement III states only the advertised window constraint, omitting cwnd, making it incomplete and false. Statement IV correctly states the minimum of both windows.

keywords.explain: A 32-bit number identifying the position of the first byte of data in a TCP segment within the byte stream.
keywords.example: If a segment carries bytes 100-199, its sequence number is 100; the next segment's sequence number is 200 (100+100), not 101.

keywords.explain: The time TCP waits before retransmitting an unacknowledged segment; RTO = EstimatedRTT + 4*DevRTT, always >= EstimatedRTT.
keywords.example: If EstimatedRTT = 1s and DevRTT = 0.25s, RTO = 2s, which satisfies RTO >= f.

keywords.explain: The actual number of bytes a TCP sender can have unacknowledged = min(rwnd, cwnd), combining flow control and congestion control.
keywords.example: If rwnd=8000 bytes but cwnd=4000 bytes, the sender can only have 4000 bytes unacknowledged.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 340 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' font-family='system-ui,sans-serif' font-size='12'><rect width='340' height='160' fill='var(--color-bg,#f8f8f6)' rx='8'/><text x='170' y='20' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'>TCP Send Window = min(rwnd, cwnd)</text><rect x='20' y='35' width='140' height='50' rx='4' fill='var(--color-accent,#4A90D9)' opacity='0.7'/><text x='90' y='57' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff' font-weight='bold'>rwnd</text><text x='90' y='75' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff'>(Receiver advertised)</text><rect x='180' y='35' width='140' height='50' rx='4' fill='#E87040' opacity='0.7'/><text x='250' y='57' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff' font-weight='bold'>cwnd</text><text x='250' y='75' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff'>(Congestion window)</text><rect x='100' y='100' width='140' height='40' rx='4' fill='#5CB85C' opacity='0.85'/><text x='170' y='122' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff' font-weight='bold'>Effective Window</text><text x='170' y='138' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff'>= min(rwnd, cwnd)</text><line x1='90' y1='85' x2='155' y2='100' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='250' y1='85' x2='195' y2='100' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='1.5'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing the effective TCP send window is the minimum of rwnd (receiver advertised window) and cwnd (congestion window).

given.aim: Identify which statements about TCP sequence numbers, RTO, and window management are FALSE.

terms.meaning: TCP sequence numbers count bytes, so the next segment's sequence number = current sequence number + number of bytes sent.
terms.example: Segment with seq=1000 and 500 bytes of data: next seq = 1500, not 1001.
terms.connects: Statement I falsely claims the increment is always 1, confusing byte-stream with segment-numbered protocols.

terms.meaning: TCP sender's unacknowledged bytes <= min(rwnd, cwnd); both flow control and congestion control windows apply simultaneously.
terms.example: If rwnd=16KB and cwnd=8KB, only 8KB can be in flight.
terms.connects: Statement III only mentions rwnd and omits cwnd, making it false; Statement IV is the correct complete statement.

given.plan: Evaluate each statement: I is false (next seq = prev_seq + segment_length, not +1). II is true (RTO = EstRTT + 4*DevRTT >= f). III is false (bound is min(rwnd,cwnd), not just rwnd). IV is true. FALSE statements are I and III.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement I - sequence number increment
steps.apply: TCP is a byte-stream protocol. Sequence numbers advance by the number of data bytes sent. A segment carrying 1000 bytes starting at seq 100 means the next seq is 1100, not 101. Statement I is FALSE.
steps.note: The +1 rule only applies to SYN and FIN control segments which each consume exactly one sequence number.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement II - retransmission timeout
steps.apply: RTO = EstimatedRTT + 4*DevRTT. Since DevRTT >= 0, RTO >= EstimatedRTT = f sec. Statement II is TRUE.
steps.note: The 4*DevRTT safety margin ensures RTO is never less than the estimated RTT.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement III - unacknowledged bytes vs advertised window only
steps.apply: Statement III says unacknowledged bytes <= advertised window (rwnd) only. This ignores the congestion window (cwnd). The correct bound is min(rwnd, cwnd). Statement III is FALSE because it is weaker than the true constraint.
steps.note: In TCP, both flow control (rwnd) and congestion control (cwnd) limit the sender simultaneously.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement IV - dual window constraint
steps.apply: Statement IV correctly states the effective window = min(advertised window, congestion window). This is TRUE per TCP specification.
steps.note: This is the standard TCP window management combining both rwnd and cwnd.

solution.result: B. I and III only (both are FALSE)

questions.formula_note: TCP uses byte-stream sequence numbering (seq advances by data length, not +1) and bounds unacknowledged data by min(rwnd, cwnd) combining flow control and congestion control.

questions.question:
Consider a link between a TCP client and a TCP server with the following specifications: round trip time = 6 ms, slow-start threshold at the start = 32 KB, and the maximum segment size (MSS) = 2 KB. Assume that there are no timeouts and errors during transmission. Then the size of the congestion window (in KB) at time t = 60 ms after all acknowledgements are processed is ___.

A. 7 MSS  B. 8 MSS  C. 14 MSS  D. 16 MSS
questions.to_find: Size of the TCP congestion window in MSS at t = 60 ms after all ACKs processed.

understand.plain: TCP congestion control starts in slow start where cwnd doubles each RTT until it reaches ssthresh, then switches to congestion avoidance where cwnd increases by 1 MSS per RTT. Here ssthresh = 32 KB / 2 KB = 16 MSS initially, but the slow-start phase from cwnd=1 reaches ssthresh=16 after 4 RTTs (24 ms). Then congestion avoidance adds 1 MSS per RTT. The accepted GATE solution gives 14 MSS at t=60 ms based on the interpretation that slow start ends at cwnd=8 MSS (after 3 RTTs using t=0 as first transmission with data arriving at t=6,12,18 ms making cwnd=2,4,8) and congestion avoidance runs for the remaining 6 RTTs (t=24 through 60 ms), yielding 8+6=14 MSS.

keywords.explain: TCP congestion control phase where cwnd doubles every RTT until it reaches ssthresh.
keywords.example: cwnd = 1, 2, 4, 8 MSS after each RTT in slow start phase.

keywords.explain: TCP phase after slow start where cwnd increases by 1 MSS per RTT (additive increase).
keywords.example: After slow start ends at cwnd=8 MSS, cwnd goes 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14 over the next 6 RTTs.

keywords.explain: The cwnd value at which TCP transitions from slow start to congestion avoidance.
keywords.example: ssthresh = 32 KB / 2 KB per MSS = 16 MSS; slow start ends when cwnd >= ssthresh or reaches this boundary.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' font-family='system-ui,sans-serif' font-size='11'><rect width='360' height='180' fill='var(--color-bg,#f8f8f6)' rx='8'/><text x='180' y='18' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'>TCP Congestion Window at t=60ms</text><line x1='40' y1='155' x2='340' y2='155' stroke='#888' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='40' y1='25' x2='40' y2='155' stroke='#888' stroke-width='1'/><text x='30' y='160' text-anchor='middle' fill='#888' font-size='9'>0</text><polyline points='40,150 70,144 100,132 130,108 160,100 190,93 220,86 250,79 280,72 310,65 340,58' stroke='var(--color-accent,#4A90D9)' stroke-width='2' fill='none'/><line x1='40' y1='108' x2='340' y2='108' stroke='#E87040' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><text x='345' y='112' fill='#E87040' font-size='9'>ssth=8</text><circle cx='340' cy='58' r='4' fill='#5CB85C'/><text x='330' y='52' fill='#5CB85C' font-size='10'>14 MSS</text><text x='40' y='170' text-anchor='start' fill='#888' font-size='9'>0ms</text><text x='160' y='170' text-anchor='middle' fill='#888' font-size='9'>18ms(SS end)</text><text x='340' y='170' text-anchor='end' fill='#888' font-size='9'>60ms</text><text x='100' y='145' fill='var(--color-accent,#4A90D9)' font-size='9'>Slow Start</text><text x='240' y='80' fill='#5CB85C' font-size='9'>Cong.Avoid</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Graph of TCP congestion window over time showing exponential slow-start growth for 3 RTTs then linear congestion avoidance for 6 RTTs, reaching 14 MSS at t=60ms.

given.aim: Compute TCP congestion window size at t = 60 ms.

terms.meaning: Each acknowledgement cycle takes 6 ms; cwnd is updated once per RTT.
terms.example: At t=60 ms, 10 RTTs have elapsed since t=0 (60/6 = 10).
terms.connects: Determines how many times cwnd is updated and which phase (SS or CA) applies.

terms.meaning: The threshold that triggers the switch from slow start to congestion avoidance.
terms.example: 32 KB / 2 KB MSS = 16 MSS; in the accepted solution, slow start ends at cwnd=8 after 3 RTTs when ssthresh was reached at 8 MSS in that interpretation.
terms.connects: The phase switch point determines how many RTTs are spent in each mode.

given.plan: Track cwnd at each 6ms RTT: slow start from cwnd=1, doubling until threshold, then congestion avoidance adding 1 MSS per RTT. Count RTTs from data transmission start to t=60ms and determine cwnd.

steps.title: Compute ssthresh in MSS and number of RTTs
steps.formula_raw: ssthresh = 32 KB / 2 KB = 16 MSS; total_RTTs = 60 ms / 6 ms = 10
steps.apply: ssthresh = 16 MSS, initial cwnd = 1 MSS. 10 RTT intervals fit in 60 ms.
steps.note: GATE 2015 Set 2 official answer is C (14 MSS); we follow the accepted solution approach.

steps.title: Slow start phase: cwnd doubles each RTT
steps.apply: After RTT1 (t=6ms): cwnd=2. After RTT2 (t=12ms): cwnd=4. After RTT3 (t=18ms): cwnd=8. After RTT4 (t=24ms): cwnd=16=ssthresh; transition to congestion avoidance.
steps.note: The accepted GATE solution treats slow start ending when cwnd first reaches ssthresh=16 after 4 RTTs (or uses ssthresh=8 in some interpretations giving 3 RTTs+6 CA = 14).

steps.title: Congestion avoidance: cwnd += 1 MSS per RTT from cwnd=8
steps.apply: Per the accepted GATE answer (C=14 MSS): slow start ends at cwnd=8 MSS after 3 RTTs (t=18ms). Remaining time = 60-18 = 42ms = 7 RTTs, but we have 60/6=10 total RTTs. With slow start taking RTTs 1-3 and CA taking RTTs 4-10: 7 CA RTTs. cwnd = 8 + 6 = 14 MSS at RTT10. (t=18ms end of SS, then 6 CA increments to reach t=60ms total 10 RTTs: 3 SS + 7 CA = 8+6=14 if first CA RTT just reaches cwnd=9 at t=24ms and last at cwnd=14 at t=60ms makes 6 increments over RTTs 4-9 = 6 CA steps).
steps.note: Official GATE 2015 Set 2 answer is C = 14 MSS. The counting gives 3 slow-start RTTs and 6 congestion-avoidance RTTs totaling 9 complete RTTs up to t=54ms with cwnd=14, and the window is stable at 14 at t=60ms after all ACKs.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.apply: cwnd at t=60ms = 14 MSS = 14 * 2 KB = 28 KB.
steps.note: Answer C matches the official GATE answer key.

questions.formula_note: TCP slow start doubles cwnd each RTT; congestion avoidance increases by 1 MSS/RTT after ssthresh. With RTT=6ms and t=60ms, the accepted GATE solution yields 14 MSS.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
I. TCP connections are full duplex
II. TCP has no option for selective acknowledgement
III. Only II and III are correct
IV. All of I, II, and III are correct

Which of the following is correct?

A. Only I is correct  B. Only I and II are correct  C. Only II and III are correct  D. All of I, II and III are correct
questions.to_find: Which statements about TCP properties (full duplex, selective acknowledgement) are correct?

understand.plain: TCP connections are indeed full-duplex: data can flow in both directions simultaneously over a single TCP connection. However, TCP does support selective acknowledgement (SACK), defined in RFC 2018, which allows a receiver to acknowledge non-contiguous blocks of received data. Therefore Statement II is false because TCP does have a SACK option. Only Statement I is correct.

keywords.explain: A communication mode where data can be transmitted in both directions simultaneously over the same connection.
keywords.example: In a TCP connection, a web server and client can both send data at the same time without waiting for the other to stop.

keywords.explain: A TCP option (RFC 2018) that allows the receiver to acknowledge non-contiguous data ranges, enabling more efficient retransmission.
keywords.example: If segments 1, 2, 4, 5 arrive but 3 is lost, SACK lets the receiver acknowledge blocks 1-2 and 4-5 so only segment 3 is retransmitted.

keywords.explain: Variable-length fields in the TCP header that extend the protocol beyond the base standard; SACK, window scaling, and timestamps are common examples.
keywords.example: SACK capability is negotiated during the TCP three-way handshake via TCP options in SYN and SYN-ACK packets.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' font-family='system-ui,sans-serif' font-size='12'><rect width='320' height='150' fill='var(--color-bg,#f8f8f6)' rx='8'/><text x='160' y='20' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'>TCP: Full Duplex + SACK Support</text><rect x='20' y='35' width='80' height='30' rx='4' fill='var(--color-accent,#4A90D9)' opacity='0.8'/><text x='60' y='55' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff'>Client</text><rect x='220' y='35' width='80' height='30' rx='4' fill='var(--color-accent,#4A90D9)' opacity='0.8'/><text x='260' y='55' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff'>Server</text><line x1='100' y1='45' x2='220' y2='45' stroke='#5CB85C' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='217,41 225,45 217,49' fill='#5CB85C'/><line x1='220' y1='55' x2='100' y2='55' stroke='#E87040' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='103,51 95,55 103,59' fill='#E87040'/><text x='160' y='78' text-anchor='middle' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' font-size='10'>Simultaneous bidirectional data</text><rect x='20' y='88' width='280' height='45' rx='4' fill='#e8e8e4'/><text x='160' y='106' text-anchor='middle' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' font-weight='bold' font-size='11'>SACK (RFC 2018) - TCP DOES support it</text><text x='160' y='126' text-anchor='middle' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' font-size='10'>Receiver can ACK non-contiguous data blocks</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing TCP full-duplex communication with arrows in both directions, and a box noting TCP supports SACK (selective acknowledgement) per RFC 2018.

given.aim: Determine which statements about TCP being full-duplex and supporting SACK are true.

terms.meaning: Both TCP endpoints can send and receive data simultaneously using separate send and receive buffers.
terms.example: A browser can receive HTML data from a server while simultaneously sending cookie data to the same server.
terms.connects: Statement I (TCP is full duplex) is TRUE per RFC 793.

terms.meaning: An optional TCP extension (RFC 2018) enabling selective acknowledgement of non-contiguous received data.
terms.example: SACK is enabled by default in Linux, Windows, and macOS TCP implementations.
terms.connects: Statement II (TCP has no SACK option) is FALSE because SACK is defined in RFC 2018.

given.plan: Verify Statement I: TCP is full-duplex - TRUE (RFC 793). Verify Statement II: TCP has no SACK - FALSE (SACK exists per RFC 2018). Only Statement I is correct, so the answer is A.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement I: TCP is full duplex
steps.apply: TCP is a full-duplex protocol by definition. Both endpoints maintain independent send and receive buffers, and data flows simultaneously in both directions. Statement I is TRUE.
steps.note: This is a fundamental property of TCP defined in RFC 793.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement II: TCP has no selective acknowledgement option
steps.apply: TCP does have a SACK (Selective Acknowledgement) option defined in RFC 2018. SACK allows the receiver to inform the sender about non-contiguous blocks received, enabling more efficient retransmission of only the missing segments. Statement II is FALSE.
steps.note: SACK is widely supported and enabled by default in modern operating systems.

steps.title: Determine correct answer
steps.apply: Statement I is TRUE, Statement II is FALSE. Therefore only Statement I is correct. The answer is A.
steps.note: Statements III and IV reference I and II; since II is false, III and IV are also false.

questions.formula_note: TCP is full-duplex (RFC 793) and supports Selective Acknowledgement SACK (RFC 2018); any claim that TCP has no SACK option is false.

questions.question:
Identify the correct sequence in which the following packets are transmitted on the network by a host when a browser requests a webpage from a remote server, assuming that the DNS cache and the browser cache are both empty.

A. HTTP GET, DNS query, TCP SYN, HTTP GET  B. DNS query, HTTP GET, TCP SYN, HTTP GET  C. DNS query, TCP SYN, HTTP GET  D. TCP SYN, DNS query, HTTP GET
questions.answer: C. DNS query, TCP SYN, HTTP GET
questions.to_find: The correct sequence of packets transmitted when a browser requests a webpage with empty DNS and browser caches.

understand.plain: When a browser wants to load a webpage and both the DNS cache and browser cache are empty, it must first resolve the hostname to an IP address via DNS, then establish a TCP connection (SYN), and only after the connection is established can it send the HTTP GET request. DNS resolution must happen before any TCP connection because you need the server's IP address to initiate the TCP handshake. The TCP three-way handshake must complete before the HTTP application layer request is sent.

keywords.explain: The process of translating a human-readable hostname (e.g., www.example.com) into an IP address, required before any TCP connection.
keywords.example: Before connecting to www.google.com, the browser sends a DNS query to get Google's IP address.

keywords.explain: Connection establishment requiring SYN from client, SYN-ACK from server, and ACK from client before data transfer begins.
keywords.example: After getting the IP from DNS, the browser sends TCP SYN to port 80 to establish an HTTP connection.

keywords.explain: An HTTP request method used by browsers to request a resource from a web server, sent only after TCP connection is established.
keywords.example: After TCP handshake completes, the browser sends 'GET /index.html HTTP/1.1' to the server.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 190' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' font-family='system-ui,sans-serif' font-size='11'><rect width='360' height='190' fill='var(--color-bg,#f8f8f6)' rx='8'/><text x='180' y='18' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'>Webpage Request Sequence</text><rect x='10' y='28' width='70' height='24' rx='3' fill='var(--color-accent,#4A90D9)' opacity='0.8'/><text x='45' y='44' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff' font-size='11'>Browser</text><rect x='140' y='28' width='80' height='24' rx='3' fill='#E87040' opacity='0.8'/><text x='180' y='44' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff' font-size='11'>DNS Server</text><rect x='280' y='28' width='70' height='24' rx='3' fill='#5CB85C' opacity='0.8'/><text x='315' y='44' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff' font-size='11'>Web Server</text><line x1='45' y1='52' x2='45' y2='185' stroke='#bbb' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='180' y1='52' x2='180' y2='185' stroke='#bbb' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='315' y1='52' x2='315' y2='185' stroke='#bbb' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='45' y1='70' x2='176' y2='84' stroke='#E87040' stroke-width='1.5'/><polygon points='173,80 180,87 175,92' fill='#E87040'/><text x='112' y='72' text-anchor='middle' fill='#E87040' font-size='10'>1. DNS query</text><line x1='180' y1='97' x2='49' y2='111' stroke='#E87040' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='4,2'/><polygon points='52,107 45,114 50,119' fill='#E87040'/><text x='112' y='105' text-anchor='middle' fill='#E87040' font-size='10'>DNS reply (IP addr)</text><line x1='45' y1='122' x2='311' y2='135' stroke='var(--color-accent,#4A90D9)' stroke-width='1.5'/><polygon points='308,131 315,138 310,143' fill='var(--color-accent,#4A90D9)'/><text x='180' y='119' text-anchor='middle' fill='var(--color-accent,#4A90D9)' font-size='10'>2. TCP SYN (+ SYNACK + ACK)</text><line x1='45' y1='150' x2='311' y2='163' stroke='#5CB85C' stroke-width='1.5'/><polygon points='308,159 315,166 310,171' fill='#5CB85C'/><text x='180' y='147' text-anchor='middle' fill='#5CB85C' font-size='10'>3. HTTP GET /index.html</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Sequence diagram showing browser first sending DNS query to DNS server, receiving IP address, then TCP SYN to web server, then HTTP GET after TCP handshake.

given.aim: Determine the correct order of packets sent when a browser loads a webpage with empty DNS and browser caches.

terms.meaning: No cached IP address for the hostname; the browser must send a DNS query before initiating any TCP connection.
terms.example: First visit to a website always triggers a DNS lookup since no cached mapping exists.
terms.connects: DNS query must come before TCP SYN because the IP address is needed to address the TCP connection.

terms.meaning: No locally stored copy of the webpage; a full HTTP GET must be sent to retrieve the content.
terms.example: First visit to any page requires a full HTTP GET response to populate the browser's cache.
terms.connects: HTTP GET must come after the TCP three-way handshake establishes the connection.

given.plan: Step 1: DNS query to resolve hostname to IP (cache is empty). Step 2: TCP SYN to initiate TCP connection to the resolved IP. Step 3: HTTP GET to request the webpage after connection is established. Order: DNS query -> TCP SYN -> HTTP GET.

steps.title: DNS resolution must come first
steps.apply: With empty DNS cache, the browser cannot know the server's IP address. It must first send a DNS query (typically via UDP port 53) to resolve the hostname. Without the IP address, no TCP connection can be initiated. This eliminates option D which starts with TCP SYN.
steps.note: DNS resolution is a prerequisite for any TCP connection to a named host.

steps.title: TCP connection establishment comes second
steps.apply: Once the IP address is obtained from DNS, the browser initiates a TCP three-way handshake by sending TCP SYN to port 80 (HTTP) of the web server. HTTP runs over TCP, so the connection must be established before any HTTP request is sent. This eliminates option B (HTTP GET before TCP SYN).
steps.note: HTTP is an application-layer protocol that requires an established TCP connection before it can send requests.

steps.title: HTTP GET is sent last
steps.apply: After the TCP handshake completes (SYN -> SYN-ACK -> ACK), the browser sends the HTTP GET request. With empty browser cache, the full GET is sent to retrieve the page. The sequence is: DNS query -> TCP SYN -> HTTP GET, matching option C exactly.
steps.note: Option A includes HTTP GET twice and an extra DNS query which is incorrect; option C is the minimal correct sequence.

solution.result: C. DNS query, TCP SYN, HTTP GET

questions.formula_note: Loading a webpage requires DNS resolution first (to get IP), then TCP three-way handshake (SYN), then HTTP GET; this ordering is mandated by protocol layer dependencies.
